ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'toast' class; no specific 'honey toast' entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — toast category approximation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated honey toast code. A000N (bread and similar products) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for honey toast identified in published terminology as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of honey toast is approximately one thick-cut slice (80g), as commonly served in Singaporean cafés and toast shops.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Toast — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Toast (cr_id F02040002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 5.8g/100g based on typical butter-spread composition consistent with HPB data for similar toast preparations; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat set to -1 as HPB does not report detailed fatty acid fractions for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Bread with Honey — analogous estimate based on white bread GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'honey toast' exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread (GI ~70) moderated by the honey component (GI ~55–58 per Sydney GI Database for pure honey), yielding a blended estimate of approximately 58 for the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 31.5g) / 100 ≈ 18.3, where available carbohydrate per 80g serving = 39.4 × 0.8 = 31.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 373mg/100g combined with high sugar (14.6g/100g) and low fibre (0.6g/100g), promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit honey toast due to its borderline-high sodium content (373mg/100g) and high sugar load, which together promote fluid retention and elevate blood pressure; if consumed, limit to a half-slice and avoid additional condiments such as condensed milk or whipped cream.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate (39.4g/100g) and sugar (14.6g/100g) from refined white bread and honey, with negligible fibre (0.6g/100g), producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation and a glycemic load of 18.3 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Honey toast is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due0 to its high glycemic load (18.3 per 80g serving), high sugar content (14.6g/100g), and minimal dietary fibre (0.6g/100g); if consumed at all, restrict to a very small portion paired with a protein-rich food to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.8g/100g from butter, sodium 373mg/100g, and high sugar 14.6g/100g, all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia, arterial stiffness, and elevated cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Honey toast presents a high cardiovascular concern due to its combination of saturated fat (~5.8g/100g from butter), elevated sodium (373mg/100g), and high sugar (14.6g/100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this food or limit intake to occasional very small portions as part of an otherwise heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey toast is a popular café-style dessert and breakfast item widely served in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of a thick-cut slice of white bread that is toasted until golden and crisp, then generously spread with butter and drizzled with honey. Some preparations also include condensed milk or a light dusting of icing sugar. The result is a warm, sweet, and buttery dish with a crisp exterior and soft, pillowy interior. Nutritionally, honey toast is energy-dense at 289 kcal per 100g, with a significant carbohydrate load of 39.4g/100g predominantly from refined white bread, and a high sugar content of 14.6g/100g contributed by both the honey and any condensed milk used. Fat content is 11.43g/100g, largely from butter, with an estimated saturated fat fraction of approximately 5.8g/100g. Dietary fibre is very low at 0.6g/100g, and sodium is elevated at 373mg/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) with a glycemic load of approximately 18.3 per standard 80g serving, making it a high-glycemic-load food in practice. Clinically, honey toast is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its combination of high sugar, borderline-high sodium, saturated fat, and low fibre. It is best regarded as an occasional treat rather than a regular dietary item.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Honey Toast (cr_id F02040002) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from typical butter-spread composition consistent with HPB data for similar preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white bread and honey component data, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for honey toast are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food item as of 2026. Saturated fat value of 5.8g/100g is an estimate based on typical butter content in similar HPB-catalogued toast preparations and is not directly reported by HPB for this specific entry. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white bread and honey entries; no direct honey toast GI measurement was found in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (58 × 31.5g) / 100 = 18.3, using an 80g serving size.
